teamObjective.explanation: At the opening lead with a very weak hand, the best team goal is to play a low card that lets partner win the trick cheaply or forces opponents to spend a higher card/trump, preserving my limited spades for later.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner likely has 3 cards and no public information about their strength. They may be able to beat a low spade 3 with a higher non-trump or a trump, but this is uncertain.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents also have 3 cards each and no public plays have been revealed. They probably can beat a spade 3 with many higher cards, but they may prefer to conserve strength unless they need to take the lead.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the lowest card (spade 3) minimizes resource loss. The 3 is almost certainly not going to win, but it gives partner the cheapest chance to take control and forces opponents to reveal at least one card if they want the lead.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 4 is only marginally stronger than the 3 and still very unlikely to win; keeping it for a later spade progression or as a slightly higher throwaway is better.
The 5 is one of my better cards and would be wasted on an opening lead where opponents or partner are likely to have higher cards.
The 6 is my strongest card but still weak overall; using it now sacrifices the only card most likely to win a low spade trick later.

risks: An opponent may win the trick with a higher card or trump and retain the lead.
Partner may not be able to help, leaving my team without control.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By playing the 3, the smallest possible commitment, I limit downside and keep 4/5/6 for future tricks where exact rank might matter.

notes: No public plays beyond new_game, so partner and opponent beliefs remain highly uncertain.
